description: Operational diagnosis + decision tasks from real Paperclip incidents (the diagnose-why-work-stopped class). Forensic root-cause tasks graded on the three liveness invariants and on naming all compounding defects, plus objective routing/escalation decisions and a pseudo-stop vs real-blocker classification.

tasks.title: Diagnose a recovery-loop and propose a bounded fix
tasks.prompt:
You are diagnosing a Paperclip liveness incident (no code changes — produce a root cause + ONE product fix).

SYMPTOM: the stranded-issue recovery routine (reconcileStrandedAssignedIssues) fires ~110 times/hour. 986 of those per day fail with "Agent is not invokable (paused)". The agents being hammered are 13 claude leadership primaries that were DELIBERATELY paused (weekly rate limit; they resume on a fixed date). The recovery loop keeps trying to re-invoke them.

Three invariants any fix MUST preserve: (1) productive work continues, (2) only REAL blockers stop work, (3) no infinite loops.

In under 150 words: (a) state the root cause, (b) propose exactly ONE product rule that fixes it, and (c) note how your fix holds all three invariants. Do not propose code.

criteria.name: correctness
criteria.guidance: Correctly roots the cause in recovery re-invoking deliberately-paused agents, and the fix is to skip/exclude paused agents from the recovery sweep (not to unpause them or widen retries). Penalize fixes that would resume the agents or just add backoff without skipping.

criteria.name: invariants
criteria.guidance: Explicitly and validly explains how the fix preserves all three invariants — especially that work still proceeds (paused is a real wait, not a silent stop) and the loop becomes bounded.

criteria.name: actionability
criteria.guidance: Proposes exactly one concrete product rule, not a vague gesture.

tasks.title: Apply the escalate-after-3 review policy
tasks.prompt:
Apply this Paperclip operating policy exactly.

POLICY: A recovery/productivity review escalates to leadership ONLY after 3 consecutive unresolved reviews. Before the 3rd, it routes to the cheap shell-handler Compiler (no escalation). Escalation happens AT MOST once per tree.

STATE: an issue tree has just completed its 3rd consecutive unresolved review. It has never been escalated before.

Return ONLY JSON: {"action": "escalate"|"route_compiler", "target": string, "reason": string}. No prose.

tasks.title: Diagnose the three compounding defects in the restart race
tasks.prompt:
Forensics on a recurring Paperclip instability (no code — root cause + plan).

SYMPTOM: every time the source server restarts, it sometimes fails to come back cleanly, and in-flight runs die. Evidence gathered:
1. launchd is configured KeepAlive=true with a short throttle, so it re-execs the start script the instant `pnpm dev` exits — but the OLD embedded-Postgres postmaster and a detached workspace-runtime on port 13100 are still alive, causing "postmaster.pid already exists" / "port 13100 in use". The start script only frees port 3100 and has no single-instance lock.
2. The SIGTERM handler doesn't drain in-flight runs or kill detached children before exit.
3. On startup, reapOrphanedRuns() is called with no stale-threshold (defaults to 0), so every still-'running' run is immediately marked process_lost.

In under 180 words, give the root cause as a SET of THREE distinct compounding defects, and ONE fix per defect. Be specific to each.

criteria.name: all_three
criteria.guidance: Identifies ALL THREE distinct defects as separate compounding causes: (a) no single-instance lock / stale postmaster+port-13100 not reaped before relaunch, (b) SIGTERM doesn't drain runs or kill detached children, (c) reapOrphanedRuns default-0 threshold instantly fails live runs. Deduct heavily for each missed defect or for collapsing them into one.

criteria.name: fixes
criteria.guidance: Gives a correct, specific fix per defect (e.g. flock/single-instance + ordered teardown of old PG/ports; graceful drain + reset in-flight to queued on SIGTERM; non-zero startup reap grace). Vague 'add error handling' scores low.

tasks.title: Choose a failover lane under quota constraints
tasks.prompt:
Decide which model lane to route a leadership agent's work to RIGHT NOW, per Paperclip's failover policy.

FAILOVER ORDER (use the first lane that is AVAILABLE): claude -> codex -> hermes(grok) -> gemini.
A lane is UNAVAILABLE if it is paused, rate-limit-exhausted, or its auth is invalidated.

CURRENT STATE:
- claude: PAUSED (weekly limit hit; primaries paused until reset).
- codex: AVAILABLE (regular codex fine).
- hermes/grok: available.
- gemini: available.

Return ONLY JSON: {"lane": "claude"|"codex"|"hermes"|"gemini", "reason": string}. Pick the correct first-available lane in the failover order. No prose.

tasks.title: Classify real blocker vs pseudo-stop
tasks.prompt:
Paperclip's liveness rule: work may stop ONLY for a REAL blocker (genuinely cannot proceed: needs board/human input, external credential, QA gate, or exhausted budget). A PSEUDO-stop is an issue sitting idle with nothing actually waiting on it (e.g. in_review with no participant/approver assigned, or a silent stall) — those must be routed back to productive work, not left stopped.

CASE: an issue is in status "in_review" for 240 hours. There is NO reviewer assigned, NO approval requested, and NO participant waiting. The underlying work is complete and nothing external is required.

Return ONLY JSON: {"classification": "real_blocker"|"pseudo_stop", "next_action": string, "reason": string}. No prose.

tasks.title: Order incidents by severity (objective triage)
tasks.prompt:
Triage these 4 live incidents into a response order (most urgent first), per this policy: a FULL outage (whole instance down) outranks a SINGLE-company outage, which outranks DEGRADED performance, which outranks a COSMETIC ui bug.

INCIDENTS:
- "ui-badge": a status badge shows the wrong color (cosmetic).
- "capital-down": ThinkStack Capital's agents can't run (single-company outage).
- "all-stalled": the whole instance stopped dispatching runs (full outage).
- "slow-board": the board loads in ~8s instead of <1s (degraded performance).

Return ONLY a JSON array of the 4 ids, most-urgent first. No prose. Shape: ["a","b","c","d"].

tasks.title: Separate root cause from downstream symptoms in a cascade
tasks.prompt:
A cascading Paperclip outage just fired five alerts within 90 seconds. Find the ONE root cause and label the rest as downstream symptoms — do not treat a symptom as the cause.

TIMELINE (timestamps in seconds):
- t=0: the embedded Postgres connection pool is exhausted — every new query blocks waiting for a connection that never frees (a slow migration grabbed and held all 20 connections).
- t=12: heartbeat.ts can't write heartbeats (its DB writes hang), so heartbeats stop landing.
- t=31: the liveness monitor sees stale heartbeats and starts marking healthy in-flight runs as process_lost.
- t=44: the board UI shows 'all agents offline' because it reads run status from the DB and every read is queued behind the exhausted pool.
- t=58: PagerDuty fires a FULL-OUTAGE page ('instance down').

The on-call's instinct is to restart the instance (which would clear heartbeats and reap even more runs) or to bump the liveness threshold. Identify the true root cause and the fix that addresses IT, not a symptom.

Return ONLY JSON: {"root_cause": string, "symptoms": [string], "fix": string, "why_not_symptom": string}. `root_cause` must name the connection-pool exhaustion / the migration holding all connections; `symptoms` must list the downstream effects (stale heartbeats, runs marked process_lost, board offline, the page); `fix` must target the pool/migration, not restart-or-raise-threshold; `why_not_symptom` <=40 words.

criteria.name: root_cause_correct
criteria.guidance: Correctly names the t=0 Postgres connection-pool exhaustion (the long migration holding all 20 connections) as THE single root cause. Heavily penalize answers that name a downstream symptom as the cause — especially stale heartbeats, runs marked process_lost, the board showing offline, or the page itself — and penalize 'the server is down' as if that were primary.

criteria.name: symptom_separation
criteria.guidance: Lists the downstream effects (heartbeats not landing, healthy runs falsely reaped as process_lost, board reads queued/offline, the full-outage page) AS symptoms of the pool exhaustion, in the right causal direction. Penalize inverting causality or mislabeling the cause as a symptom.

criteria.name: fix_targets_cause
criteria.guidance: The fix addresses the pool/migration (kill or throttle the migration so it releases connections / run it without holding the whole pool, add a connection cap or statement timeout so one job can't starve the pool). Heavily penalize the trap fixes: restarting the instance (clears heartbeats and reaps MORE runs) or just raising the liveness threshold (masks the symptom while the pool stays exhausted).

tasks.title: Decide if a 3am page is actionable or noise
tasks.prompt:
It is 3am. A page just woke the on-call. Decide whether this is ACTIONABLE (a human/agent must act now) or NOISE (auto-recovering or expected; should not have paged) — then make the correct call.

POLICY: page only for conditions that (a) are not self-healing AND (b) need intervention now. Transient conditions that the system recovers from on its own, or expected/known-paused states, are NOISE and the alert rule should be fixed, not the system.

PAGE DETAILS: alert 'agent-invoke-failed' fired because 13 claude leadership primaries returned 'Agent is not invokable (paused)'. These 13 are the DELIBERATELY paused weekly-rate-limited primaries that auto-resume on their fixed reset date (in 2 days). Productive work is still flowing on the codex and grok lanes; no customer-facing capability is down; nothing is stuck waiting on these 13. The same alert has fired every night this week at the same volume.

Return ONLY JSON: {"verdict": "actionable"|"noise", "page_now": true|false, "fix": string, "reason": string}. `page_now` is whether a human should be woken for THIS condition. `fix` says what to change so this stops mis-paging. `reason` <=40 words.

criteria.name: call_correct
criteria.guidance: Classifies this as NOISE / non-actionable: the paused primaries are an expected, self-resolving state (auto-resume in 2 days), work is still flowing on other lanes, nothing is blocked, and the alert repeats nightly. No human should be woken. Heavily penalize 'actionable'/page_now=true, and penalize any answer that proposes unpausing the rate-limited primaries (that fights the deliberate pause) or treats steady productive work as an emergency.

criteria.name: fix_quality
criteria.guidance: The fix corrects the ALERT, not the system: suppress/exclude deliberately-paused agents from this alert rule, alert only when paused-state actually blocks work or when no lane is making progress, or silence until the known reset date. Penalize fixes that page louder, that add no rule change, or that 'fix' it by waking someone.

tasks.title: Roll back vs forward-fix a bad deploy
tasks.prompt:
A deploy 25 minutes ago is actively losing data, and you must choose: ROLL BACK to the previous release now, or FORWARD-FIX (ship a patch on top).

FACTS:
- The new release writes agent run results to a column that the migration RENAMED; the rename half-applied, so ~every 3rd run's result is being dropped (silent data loss, happening right now and unrecoverable once the run is gc'd).
- The previous release is known-good and is still deployed-and-runnable (blue/green: the old version is one switch away, ~30 seconds to cut back).
- The forward-fix is NOT written yet; best estimate is 40+ minutes to write, review, and deploy safely, and it touches the same migration that is already half-applied.
- The migration is ADDITIVE-compatible: the old release runs fine against the current schema (the renamed column is additive; the old code reads the original column, which still exists).

Choose the action that stops the bleeding fastest with the least risk. Return ONLY JSON: {"action": "roll_back"|"forward_fix", "reason": string, "followup": string}. `reason` <=45 words must weigh time-to-stop-bleeding and risk; `followup` says what happens after.

criteria.name: decision_correct
criteria.guidance: Chooses ROLL BACK: data is being lost right now and unrecoverably, the previous release is known-good and one ~30s switch away, and it is schema-compatible with the current (additive) migration — so rollback stops the bleeding in seconds at minimal risk. Heavily penalize forward_fix: it leaves data loss running for 40+ minutes AND touches the already-half-applied migration (higher risk). Penalize any answer that ignores the active, unrecoverable data loss or that doubts rollback safety despite the stated blue/green + additive-compat facts.

criteria.name: followup_quality
criteria.guidance: After rolling back, the followup is sane: stabilize on the known-good release, then fix the half-applied rename / build and test the forward fix off the hot path, redeploy carefully, and (bonus) audit/quantify the runs already lost. Penalize 'roll back and immediately redeploy the same broken release', or a followup that never fixes the underlying migration.

tasks.title: Order the recovery runbook steps correctly
tasks.prompt:
The Paperclip source server died mid-flight and you are bringing it back. Put these six recovery steps into the correct safe order. Ordering rules that MUST hold:
- You must STOP the flapping supervisor (launchd KeepAlive is re-execing the start script every few seconds) BEFORE anything else, or each step races a relaunch.
- You must kill the stale embedded-Postgres postmaster and free port 13100 (old workspace-runtime) BEFORE you start the new process, or it crashes on 'postmaster.pid exists' / 'port in use'.
- You must start the app and let it pass a health check BEFORE you reconcile/reap runs, so reaping runs against a live DB with a real heartbeat grace (not the 0-default that instantly fails live runs).
- You must verify productive work has resumed (runs dispatching, heartbeats landing) as the FINAL step.
- Re-enabling the launchd supervisor must happen AFTER the app is confirmed healthy, never before.

STEPS (label = id):
- "reap": reconcile/reap orphaned runs with a non-zero stale-threshold grace.
- "verify": confirm runs are dispatching and heartbeats are landing.
- "start": start the app (pnpm dev) and wait for the health check to pass.
- "clean": kill the stale postmaster and free port 13100.
- "halt-supervisor": disable launchd KeepAlive so it stops re-execing the start script.
- "reenable-supervisor": re-enable launchd KeepAlive.

Return ONLY a JSON array of the 6 ids in execution order, first step first. No prose. Shape: ["a","b","c","d","e","f"].
